worksheets.name: Asterisk
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text:
The Pulse Score, based on the Sustainable Apparel Coalition's Higg Index, is a global measure of sustainability in the fashion industry. It encompasses data from both large and small to medium-sized companies, offering a comprehensive view of industry-wide sustainability performance.Æ

Measured on a scale from   1−100Æ

Overall, the Pulse Score of the fashion industry is: 42 / 100 (2019)Æ

Specific industries are listed below, the length of the bar show pulse score whereas the width of each bar signifies the size of market share in the entire fashion industry.

worksheets.name: CO2
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text:
33.4 kg of CO2 released during it's lifetimeÆ
They can be split out into 6 stages, hover over the jeans to find out more about how it's distributed..

worksheets.name: Cotton
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text:
20% fabric waste in denim production.Æ
Denim waste often ends up in landfills or is incinerated, adding to greenhouse gas emissions and environmental harm.

worksheets.name: PROB
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text:
The fashion industry has faced criticism for its disregard of social and environmental concerns, with its significant contribution to CO2 emissions, water consumption, energy consumption, and textile waste. Æ

This has been exacerbated by the rise of fast fashion, characterized by frequent, low-priced clothing turnover. As consumption and production have surged, driven by low prices and high turnover, clothing has become more disposable, leading to environmental degradation. Æ

Shown by the graph, by the 2010s, textile-production growth overtook world-population growth, largely driven by the rise of fast fashion.Æ

Throughout this viz, try to hover over everything you see in order to gain some more information about the chart.

worksheets.name: R Chem
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text: Phosphorus fertilizer use exceeds safe limits by over 220%. The fashion industry, particularly cotton production, is a major contributor, using 4% of global fertilizers. This causes harmful runoff and algal blooms. Cotton, on 3% of agricultural land, consumes 16% of insecticides and 7% of herbicides. Toxic discharges from processing plants harm the environment and health, leading to conditions like cancer. Better chemical management by 2030 could save €7 billion annually by reducing health impacts.

worksheets.name: R ENergy
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text: Current atmospheric CO2 levels exceed safe limits by 20%, with fashion industry emissions projected to rise over 60% to 2.8 billion tons annually by 2030. Improved energy management in the fashion industry could save the global economy €67 billion. Key manufacturing locations are highly vulnerable to climate change, making efficient energy use crucial.

worksheets.name: R Land
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text: Deforestation for cotton cultivation exceeds safe limits by 17%. By 2030, the fashion industry will need 35% more land, totaling over 115 million hectares, for cotton, fibers, and livestock. This land is crucial for food crops or forest preservation. With a global population of 8.5 billion by 2030, food production must increase by 60%, creating a conflict between textile and food production. The fashion industry must adopt less land-intensive materials to avoid higher land costs and restricted access.

worksheets.name: R Waste
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text: Humankind produces 2.1 billion tons of waste annually, exceeding the Earth's absorption capacity by 1.6 times. The fashion industry's waste is projected to increase by 60% from 2015 to 2030, adding 57Æ million tons annually, reaching 148Æ million tons by 2030. This equals 17.5 kg of waste per person per year. Most clothing waste is landfilled or incinerated, with only 20% collected for reuse or recycling globally.

worksheets.name: R Water
worksheets.customized_tooltip_text: The fashion industry consumes 79 billion cubic meters of water annually, projected to increase by 50% by 2030. This is critical as major cotton producers like China and India already face high water stress. By 2030, the water demand-supply gap could reach 40%, forcing a choice between cotton production and clean drinking water. Maintaining current water use levels in the fashion industry could save the global economy €32 billion annually.

11 data sources form an INDEPENDENT INFOGRAPHIC CONSTELLATION with ZERO genuine Tableau data blends. Every worksheet binds exactly one real federated.* source; the parser's 'multi-source' flags are all Parameters pseudo-source references (the [Parameter 3] sheet-swap), not blends. Dashboard has only 3 edit-parameter-action (sheet-swap nav) and no cross-worksheet filter/highlight actions. Modeled 9 sources as independent tables + 2 explicit many->one relationships.

2 relationships built from the Tableau geometry-table + value-table blend idiom, made explicit as physical joins: Jeans Polygon[Shape Id]->JEANS[ID] (verified 8 unique ids, 0 unmatched) and Polygon water[Category]->JEANS water[Category] (verified 4 unique cats, 0 unmatched). Both many->one, single-direction (dim->detail). Tableau blend aggregates-then-links; reproduced safely because each 'one' side is a unique-key value table (no fan-out).

PARSER GAP (repo-wide): all 35 calculated-field formulas are null in migration-spec.json (kind='calculated' but formula absent). Recovered every formula from the source .twb <calculation class='tableau' formula='...'> XML before translating.

PARSER GAP: worksheets are over-reported as 'multi-source' when the only additional source is the Parameters pseudo-source. Recommend excluding 'Parameters' from source-count / blend detection to avoid false-positive blends.

Data source 'Radial (Radar Environmental)' is VESTIGIAL - no worksheet binds it (leftover duplicate of the live 'Radial with Borders'). Kept as an isolated table for completeness; all 10 of its calcs dropped.
SOURCE DATA TYPO: Impact Area member 'Waste creation (Mn rons)' should read 'Mn tons' (cf. 'Energy emissions (Mn tons)'). Preserved faithfully as a data value; flag to customer.
Calculated-field caption typo 'Point X FLipped' (capital L) normalized to 'Point X Flipped' for cross-table naming consistency with the other two mirror columns; flag to customer.

CAPABILITY NOTE for pbi-report-builder: the marimekko cumulative column-width (RUNNING_SUM 'Calculation1' + 'Running Total X Axis' table calcs) and the radar's polar->cartesian scatter need a custom/marimekko visual and a scatter-line visual respectively. Model provides the building blocks: Segment Pulse LOD measure, Distance (r) measure, and Angle/X-axis/Y-axis calc columns.

Angle reproduces Tableau RUNNING_SUM((2*PI)/COUNTD(Impact Area)) as (2*PI/DISTINCTCOUNT)*RANKX(ALL(Impact Area),ASC,Dense). ORDERING ASSUMPTION: alphabetical Impact-Area rank; this only rotates the radar (spoke assignment), polygon shape/values unchanged. RANKX-on-text tie-breaking passes offline dual-method ground truth but is only fully confirmed on a live engine.

Tableau X-axis/Y-axis = Distance(r)*COS/SIN(Angle) where Distance(r)=AVG(Radial Value). Modeled as row-level calc columns substituting the base Radial Value for the aggregate - valid because Radial Value is constant per (Impact Area, Year) mark (verified). Revisit as a measure if a future extract varies Radial Value within a mark.

Mixed-content columns typed text to avoid refresh nulling: Radial with Borders[Actual Value] (blanks + numbers). Marimekko[Actual Pulse Score] Tableau-declared string but all clean ints duplicating Pulse Score -> typed int64, summarizeBy none. Report should treat Actual Value as a label, not a measure.

AI/Copilot-readiness pass DONE: business-meaning descriptions set on all 12 tables, 70 columns, 6 measures (100% coverage), with enum domains listed on every categorical column (e.g. Marimekko[Segment], Performance Quartile[Chain Stage], Ripped Polygon[Importance]). Applied via powerbi-modeling-mcp offline (ConnectFolder->Update->ExportToTmdlFolder), no Power BI Desktop needed.

Skipped (not committable via Git/TMDL today, per agent instructions): AI data schema + AI instructions (LSDL - no stable file contract), verified answers (not Git-supported), and Approved-for-Copilot / indexing (Fabric service settings, not model files). Revisit when a file-level contract exists.

CONCURRENCY GOTCHA: the powerbi-modeling-mcp connection registry is GLOBAL/shared across parallel agent sessions. Its 'uses the last connection if omitted' default is RACY - during this build two sibling migrations (spiraling-satellites, interactive-resume) connected concurrently and a table Update with no connectionName resolved against a sibling's model ('table not found', transaction rolled back cleanly - no cross-model write). MUST pin connectionName on EVERY call when builds run in parallel.

ExportToTmdlFolder cosmetically normalizes model-wide: relationships drop explicit fromCardinality:many/toCardinality:one/crossFilteringBehavior:oneDirection lines when they equal TOM defaults (semantically identical - deserializes back to Many->One OneDirection, verified), and simple identifiers are unquoted (column Segment vs column 'Segment'). Offline validators that parse TMDL must be quoting-agnostic. lineageTags, DAX, summarizeBy, and descriptions are all preserved.
